examples.english: will certainly eat from ˹the fruit of˺ the trees of Zaqqûm.

senses.gloss: Egyptian balsam (Balanites aegyptiacus fruit and tree)

examples.text: المعسكر الذي تقبع به سلطات قطر وتركيا وإيران في النازل لا الطالع، تحصد الزقوم وتتجرع العلقم وتضرس الحصرم على أسنان نخرة.
examples.english: The camp in which the powers of Qaṭar, Turkey, and Iran trumpet is descending, not ascending; they reap the Egyptian balsam, gorge the colocynth, and chomp on sour grapes high-nosed.

senses.gloss: spurge (Euphorbia spp., including Euphorbia neriifolia, Euphorbia resinifera, Euphorbia abyssinica)

etymology.text: From the root ز ق م (z q m) related to gulping.

body.text: (M, K;) in the dial. of Ifreekeeyeh: (M, TA:) or
a certain food of the Arabs, in which are dates and fresh butter.
food of the people of the fire
(K:) [respecting which] I'Ab says that when the saying [in the Kur xliv. 43 and 44]
] was revealed, Aboo-Jahl said, “Dates and fresh butter: we will swallow it leisurely:” therefore God revealed [these other words of the Kur, xxxvii. 62 and 63,]

body.text_ar: تَخْرُجُ فِى اَصْلِ ا@لْجَحِيمِ طَلْعُهَا كَاَنَّهُ رُوُوسُ ا@لشَّيَاطِينِ

body.text: Verily it is a tree coming forth in the bottom of Hell, the fruit thereof
as though it were the heads of the devils,
of terrible serpents, foul in aspect, having manes,
as expl. by Bd]: (S:) it is thus called after a tree of which a description here follows. (Bd in xxxvii. 60.) ―         -b4-
A certain tree having small leaves, stinking
(Bd ubi suprà:) AHn says, (S, TA, [but this passage is only in one of my two copies of the S,]) on the authority of an Arab of the desert, of Azd es-Saráh, that the
having small round leaves, without thorns,
and bitter, having knots in its stems,
many in number, and a small and very weak flower, which the bees eat,
its flower is white; and the heads of its leaves are very foul,
a certain plant in the desert
having a flower resembling in form the jasmine.

the Ghowr, having a fruit like the date, sweet, with an astringent and bitter quality; the stone of which has an oil greatly esteemed for its beneficial properties, wonderful of operation in dispersing the cold kinds of flatus, and phlegmatic disorders, and pains of the joints, and gout in the foot
and sciatica, and the flatus that is confined in the socket of the hip: the weight of seven drachms thereof is drunk three days or five days; and sometimes,
often, it makes to rise and stand the crippled and those who are deprived of the power of motion:
it is said that its original was the [species of]
, which the Benoo-Umeiyeh removed (from India, TA), and planted in Areehà; and when it had long remained, the soil of Areehà altered it from the natural character of the
